aliases: Cottage Cheese Dumplings in Cream Sauce

english: Cottage Cheese Dumplings in Cream Sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Malai Kofta exists; mapped to the broader 'curry dish' class as the closest defensible ancestor. A more specific term for Indian vegetarian dumpling curry is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Malai Kofta. A0CKP (Vegetable dishes) is the closest defensible category given the primary vegetable and paneer dumpling composition simmered in a cream-based sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for Malai Kofta or equivalent Indian dumpling curry found in the clinical terminology. SNOMED CT is not the appropriate ontology for this food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Malai Kofta is approximately 300 g, comprising 4–6 paneer and vegetable dumplings submerged in a generous portion of creamy almond-tomato sauce, served alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Malai Kofta — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, ICMR-NIN, and USDA FoodData Central component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Malai Kofta as a composite dish. Values are estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) component data for paneer, mixed vegetables, cream, almond paste, and ghee, combined with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cashew paste and tomato-onion gravy base. The resulting per-100 g profile reflects a typical restaurant-style Malai Kofta with a cream-rich sauce. Confidence is medium due to recipe variability across restaurants.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable and paneer curry — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable curry and paneer-based dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Malai Kofta is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous entries: mixed vegetable curry (GI ~45–50, University of Sydney GI Database) and the moderating effect of high fat and protein content from cream, paneer, and almond paste, which slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 25.8) / 100 ≈ 11.6, rounded to 11.5. Serving carbohydrate = 8.6 g/100 g × 300 g = 25.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~930 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single restaurant serving of Malai Kofta contributes approximately 930 mg sodium, representing about 40% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to half a serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~11.5 per serving) offset by high fat and protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Malai Kofta has a low estimated GI (~45) due to the fat and protein from cream, paneer, and almonds slowing glucose absorption, but the moderate glycemic load per restaurant serving and high caloric density from saturated fat warrant portion control and pairing with a low-GI staple such as basmati rice or whole-wheat roti for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~5.1 g/100 g; ~15.3 g per serving) from cream and ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A restaurant serving of Malai Kofta provides approximately 15 g of saturated fat, exceeding the American Heart Association's recommended daily limit of 13 g for a 2000 kcal diet; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional intake and request preparation with reduced cream or substitution of low-fat yogurt for heavy cream.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Malai Kofta is a classic North Indian vegetarian curry dish consisting of soft, deep-fried dumplings (koftas) made from a mixture of paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) and mashed mixed vegetables such as potato, carrot, and peas, served in a rich, velvety sauce prepared from cream, almond paste, cashew paste, tomato, onion, and aromatic spices including cardamom, cumin, coriander, and garam masala. The word 'malai' means cream in Hindi, reflecting the luxuriously creamy character of the sauce. The koftas are golden-brown on the outside and tender inside, absorbing the fragrant sauce. Nutritionally, Malai Kofta is energy-dense (~148 kcal/100 g) with a notable fat content (~10.8 g/100 g), predominantly from saturated fats in cream and ghee, and provides moderate protein (~5.2 g/100 g) from paneer. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index (~45) due to the fat and protein content moderating glucose absorption, making it less glycemically aggressive than plain rice-based dishes, though the high saturated fat and sodium content are clinically relevant for individuals managing cardiovascular disease or hypertension. Malai Kofta is a popular celebratory and restaurant dish across North India and the Indian diaspora in Singapore, commonly served with naan, roti, or basmati rice. It is inherently vegetarian and gluten-free, though not vegan due to dairy content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Malai Kofta?
Is Malai Kofta good for diabetics?
What is Malai Kofta made of?
Can I eat Malai Kofta with high blood pressure?
meal_context: paired with naan or basmati rice

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or USDA entry exists for Malai Kofta as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level data in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using comparable vegetable curry entries. Confidence is medium due to significant recipe variability across restaurants, particularly in cream, ghee, and nut paste quantities.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from ICMR-NIN (2017) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; no single approved-source entry for Malai Kofta as a composite dish exists. Values should be validated against laboratory analysis if used for clinical dietary planning. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA component sources used for this estimation; dairy-derived vitamin D contribution is negligible and not separately quantified. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for the component ingredients used in this estimation. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~45) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed vegetable curry; no direct GI measurement for Malai Kofta exists in the database. 5. The food_name has been normalised from the input 'Curry Village- Malai Kofta (Chef Recommendation)' to the canonical English name 'Malai Kofta'; the restaurant branding and chef recommendation qualifier are preserved in aliases. 6. Chinese local names (马来可夫塔 / 馬來可夫塔) are phonetic transliterations used in Singapore Chinese-language menus; no established semantic Chinese name exists for this dish. 7. Serving size of 300 g is estimated based on typical North Indian restaurant curry portions in Singapore; actual serving weights vary by restaurant.
